title: Numeracy rate in women
subtitle: Share of women aged 16-65 years who can apply multiple steps involving calculation with common decimals, percentages, and fractions; simple measurement and spatial representation; and interpretation of relatively simple data and statistics in texts, tables, and graphs.
note: Numerical proficiency at this level includes the application of multiple steps involving calculation with common decimals, percentages and fractions; simple measurement and spatial representation; and interpretation of relatively simple data and statistics in texts, tables and graphs
caption:
Numeracy rate in women

Share of women aged 16-65 years who can apply multiple steps involving calculation with common decimals, percentages, and fractions; simple measurement and spatial representation; and interpretation of relatively simple data and statistics in texts, tables, and graphs.

This choropleth world map displays the numeracy rate in women for the year 2017, measuring the share of women aged 16-65 years who possess specific numerical proficiency. The legend defines this proficiency as the ability to apply multiple steps involving calculation with common decimals, percentages, and fractions, alongside simple measurement, spatial representation, and the interpretation of data in texts and graphs. The color scale ranges from 0% to 100%, transitioning from pale yellow through green and teal to dark blue. The darkest blue shades, representing rates between 80% and 100%, are visible across Russia, much of Northern and Western Europe, Canada, and Australia. Countries such as the United States, Mexico, and Argentina appear in mid-range teal and green shades, indicating values roughly between 40% and 80%. A distinct band of lighter green is visible across parts of South America, including Colombia and Peru, suggesting rates in the 20% to 40% range. Large portions of the African continent, the Middle East, and Central Asia are covered in diagonal hatching, which corresponds to the "No data" category in the legend. The visual distribution shows the highest recorded numeracy rates concentrated in Northern Eurasia, North America, and Oceania, while significant gaps in data availability exist across Africa and parts of Asia.
